protocols.adult_dose: NAC Old Regimen: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1hr, then 50mg/kg in 500ml 5% Dextrose IV over 4hrs, then 100mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 16hrs | NAC SNAP Protocol: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2hrs, then 200mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 10hrs
protocols.notes: Toxic dose: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults | Use Paracetamol Treatment Nomogram to establish need for antidote | Do NOT withhold even if ingestion was 48-72hrs prior | Activated charcoal 50g PO if presentation <1hr post-ingestion | Paracetamol levels only useful if >4hrs post-ingestion | Start treatment preferably <8hrs post-ingestion | If in doubt, give NAC
protocols.notes_updates: Toxic dose: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults | NAC Old Regimen: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1hr, then 50mg/kg in 500ml over 4hrs, then 100mg/kg in 1L over 16hrs | SNAP Protocol: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2hrs, then 200mg/kg in 1L over 10hrs | Give activated charcoal 50g PO if <1hr post-ingestion | Paracetamol levels only if >4hrs post-ingestion | Do NOT withhold NAC even if 48-72hrs post-ingestion | Cautions: anaphylaxis, vomiting | High-risk patients: hypotensive, pre-existing liver dysfunction, elderly | Pregnancy: paracetamol crosses placenta, administer NAC as above

management_steps.details: Monitor glucose, quantify amount ingested and co-ingestants, establish timeframe
If presentation <1 hour from ingestion. Not via NGT.
If any co-ingestants or unknown substances
If above treatment line on nomogram OR toxic dose ingested OR unknown time/amount. Do not delay if >8hrs post-ingestion.
If commenced on NAC, check LFTs and coagulation

protocols.adult_dose: Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4%: 1-2mmol/kg IV bolus (repeat until QRS <120ms or pH >7.55)
protocols.notes: Sodium Bicarbonate is antidote | Seizures: Lorazepam 4mg IV (avoid Phenytoin) | Do NOT give anti-arrhythmics (Class IA/IC) | Indications for NaHCO3: QRS >100ms, arrhythmia, hypotension, seizures | Goal: arterial pH 7.50-7.55 | Ventricular arrhythmias usually respond to NaHCO3 alone
protocols.notes_updates: Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4%: 1-2mmol/kg IV bolus, repeat until QRS narrows <120ms or pH >7.55 | Seizures: Lorazepam (avoid Phenytoin - exacerbates cardiotoxicity) | Indications for bicarbonate: QRS >100ms, arrhythmias, hypotension, seizures | Goal pH: 7.50-7.55 | Do NOT give Class IA/IC anti-arrhythmics | Supportive care: intubation if GCS <8, activated charcoal if within 1hr

diagnostic_criteria.ecg_findings: QRS >100ms, terminal R wave in aVR, ventricular arrhythmias

management_steps.details: Repeat boluses until QRS <120ms or pH >7.55

protocols.adult_dose: Atropine: 2mg IV every 2-5min until dry secretions and HR >80 | Pralidoxime: 30mg/kg IV over 20min, then 8-10mg/kg/hr infusion
protocols.notes: Atropine: titrate to effect (dry secretions, HR >80). Can require massive doses. | Pralidoxime: reactivates acetylcholinesterase | Indications for pralidoxime: muscle weakness, fasciculations, respiratory depression | Remove contaminated clothing, wash skin | Do NOT give morphine/theophylline/phenothiazines/beta-blockers
protocols.notes_updates: Atropine: 2mg IV q2-5min, titrate to dry secretions and HR >80 (may need 100+ mg) | Pralidoxime: 30mg/kg IV over 20min, then 8-10mg/kg/hr infusion | Reactivates acetylcholinesterase | Indications: muscle weakness, fasciculations, respiratory depression | Remove clothes, decontaminate skin | Contraindicated: Morphine, theophylline, phenothiazines, beta-blockers | Endotracheal intubation often required

diagnostic_criteria.symptoms: SLUDGE: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI upset, Emesis; DUMBELS: Diarrhoea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation
diagnostic_criteria.cholinergic_crisis: Muscle weakness, fasciculations, respiratory failure, bronchorrhoea, miosis

management_steps.details: Remove clothing, wash skin, activated charcoal if oral ingestion <1hr
Repeat every 2-5min until secretions dry and HR >80

protocols.adult_dose: Glucagon: 5-10mg IV bolus, then 1-10mg/hr infusion | High-dose Insulin: 1 unit/kg IV bolus, then 1-10 units/kg/hr | Adrenaline: 0.1-1 ug/kg/min IV
protocols.paediatric_dose: Glucagon: 0.05-0.15mg/kg IV (max 10mg) | High-dose insulin: 1 unit/kg bolus, then 1-10 units/kg/hr
protocols.notes: Glucagon: primary antidote. Hyperglycaemia expected. | High-dose insulin therapy (HIT) + IV lipids emulsion (ILE) for refractory cases | Calcium: 1g CaCl2 or 3g CaGluconate IV if QRS widened | Glucose: maintain euglycaemia with dextrose infusion | Cardiac pacing if bradycardia unresponsive | Veno-arterial ECMO as last resort
protocols.notes_updates: Glucagon: 5-10mg IV bolus, then 1-10mg/hr infusion (primary antidote) | High-dose insulin: 1 unit/kg bolus, then 1-10 units/kg/hr infusion | IV lipid emulsion (ILE) 1.5ml/kg 20% Intralipid bolus for refractory cases | Calcium if QRS widened | Glucose management essential with high-dose insulin | Cardiac pacing if refractory bradycardia | VA-ECMO as salvage therapy
protocols.antidote_treatment: Glucagon (first-line), High-dose Insulin, IV Lipid Emulsion

diagnostic_criteria.features: Bradycardia, hypotension, hypoglycaemia, bronchospasm, seizures, cardiac arrest

management_steps.details: Bolus, then 1-10 units/kg/hr infusion + dextrose to maintain euglycaemia
1g CaCl2 or 3g CaGluconate IV if indicated

protocols.adult_dose: Calcium: 1g CaCl2 or 3g CaGluconate IV (repeat prn) | High-dose Insulin: 1 unit/kg bolus, then 1-10 units/kg/hr | Glucagon: 5-10mg IV
protocols.paediatric_dose: Calcium: 0.2ml/kg CaCl2 10% IV | High-dose insulin: 1 unit/kg bolus, then 1-10 units/kg/hr
protocols.notes: Calcium: repeated boluses may be needed. Watch for hypercalcaemia. | High-dose insulin therapy + IV lipids for refractory cases | Noradrenaline/Adrenaline for BP support | VA-ECMO as last resort | Avoid beta-blockers (negative inotropy)
protocols.notes_updates: Calcium: 1g CaCl2 or 3g CaGluconate IV, repeat as needed | High-dose insulin: 1 unit/kg bolus, then 1-10 units/kg/hr + dextrose | IV lipid emulsion for refractory shock | Vasopressors (Noradrenaline/Adrenaline) for BP | VA-ECMO as salvage | Avoid beta-blockers
protocols.antidote_treatment: Calcium, High-dose Insulin, IV Lipid Emulsion

diagnostic_criteria.features: Bradycardia, hypotension, hyperglycaemia (due to blocked insulin release), cardiac arrest

management_steps.details: Repeat as needed, monitor calcium levels

protocols.adult_dose: Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind): Number of vials = (Serum digoxin ng/ml x weight kg) / 100
protocols.notes: Indications for Digibind: K+ >5mmol/L (acute), K+ >4mmol/L (chronic), arrhythmias, hemodynamic instability, ingestion >10mg (adult) or >4mg (child) | Correct electrolytes (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) | Avoid calcium (risk of stone heart) | Bradycardia: Atropine 0.5mg IV | Ventricular arrhythmias: Lignocaine or Phenytoin | Consider pacing
protocols.notes_updates: Digibind: vials = (serum digoxin ng/ml x weight kg) / 100 | Indications: K+ >5mmol/L acute, K+ >4mmol/L chronic, arrhythmias, hemodynamic instability, ingestion >10mg adult or >4mg child | Do NOT give IV calcium (stone heart risk) | Correct K+, Mg2+ | Atropine for bradycardia | Lignocaine/Phenytoin for ventricular arrhythmias | Each vial Digibind binds 0.5mg digoxin

diagnostic_criteria.features: Nausea, vomiting, confusion, visual changes (yellow/green halos), arrhythmias, hyperkalaemia

management_steps.details: Vials = (serum digoxin ng/ml x weight kg) / 100

protocols.adult_dose: Flumazenil: 0.2mg IV over 30sec, then 0.3mg IV, then 0.5mg IV q1min (max 3mg total)
protocols.notes: Flumazenil is antidote. Caution: may precipitate seizures in chronic BZD users or if co-ingestion of pro-convulsants (TCA, cocaine) | Supportive care usually sufficient | Protect airway | If apnoeic: ventilate with bag-valve-mask, prepare for intubation | Do NOT give if seizure risk or chronic use
protocols.notes_updates: Flumazenil: 0.2mg IV over 30sec, then 0.3mg, then 0.5mg q1min (max 3mg) | CONTRAINDICATIONS: chronic BZD use (seizure risk), co-ingestion of pro-convulsants (TCA, cocaine), epilepsy | Supportive care often sufficient | Airway protection paramount | If apnoeic: BVM ventilation, prepare intubation | Do NOT use flumazenil as diagnostic tool

diagnostic_criteria.features: Sedation, respiratory depression, ataxia, confusion, coma (rarely fatal if BZD alone)

management_steps.details: Chronic BZD use, seizure history, co-ingestants
Observation if no contraindications to flumazenil

protocols.adult_dose: Naloxone: 0.4-0.8mg IV/IM (repeat q2-3min, max 10mg) | Infusion: 2/3 of effective dose per hour
protocols.notes: Naloxone is antidote. Short half-life - may need repeat doses or infusion | BVM ventilation if apnoeic | Caution: precipitating acute withdrawal in chronic users | For long-acting opioids (methadone): prolonged observation and infusion needed | Pulmonary oedema can occur post-naloxone | Intubation if GCS <8 despite naloxone
protocols.notes_updates: Naloxone: 0.4-0.8mg IV/IM, repeat q2-3min to max 10mg | Infusion: 2/3 of effective reversal dose per hour | Short half-life (30-60min) - monitor for re-narcotisation | BVM ventilation if apnoeic | Caution with chronic users (precipitates withdrawal) | Long-acting opioids require prolonged observation/infusion | Pulmonary oedema risk post-naloxone | Intubation if GCS <8 despite treatment

diagnostic_criteria.features: Respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, sedation, hypotonia, reduced consciousness
diagnostic_criteria.toxidrome: Classic triad: respiratory depression + pinpoint pupils + sedation

management_steps.details: If apnoeic, ventilate first before naloxone
2/3 of effective dose per hour if recurrent sedation

protocols.notes: Deferoxamine is antidote. X-ray may show iron tablets | Toxic dose: >20mg/kg elemental iron | Stages: (1) GI (0-6hrs), (2) Latent (6-24hrs), (3) Metabolic acidosis/shock (12-48hrs), (4) Hepatic necrosis (2-5 days), (5) GI scarring (2-8 weeks) | Whole bowel irrigation if X-ray positive | Indications for deferoxamine: systemic toxicity, metabolic acidosis, serum iron >300mcg/dL, WBC >15,000, severe GI symptoms | Stop deferoxamine when asymptomatic and iron level normal | Vin rose urine confirms chelation
protocols.notes_updates: Deferoxamine: 15mg/kg/hr IV infusion (max 6g/24hrs) | Toxic dose: >20mg/kg elemental iron | Stages: GI (0-6hrs) -> Latent (6-24hrs) -> Metabolic acidosis/shock (12-48hrs) -> Hepatic necrosis (2-5 days) -> GI scarring (2-8 weeks) | X-ray for radiopaque tablets | Whole bowel irrigation if X-ray positive | Indications for deferoxamine: systemic toxicity, acidosis, iron >300mcg/dL, WBC >15k, severe GI symptoms | Vin rose urine = chelation confirmed | Do NOT give if asymptomatic with iron <300mcg/dL

diagnostic_criteria.stages: 1. GI (0-6hrs), 2. Latent (6-24hrs), 3. Shock/Acidosis (12-48hrs), 4. Hepatic necrosis (2-5 days), 5. GI scarring (2-8 weeks)
diagnostic_criteria.indications_for_antidote: Systemic toxicity, metabolic acidosis, serum iron >300mcg/dL, WBC >15,000, severe GI symptoms

management_steps.details: If indicated. Vin rose urine confirms chelation.
Hepatic failure, GI bleeding, bowel obstruction

protocols.adult_dose: Normal Saline IV hydration | Haemodialysis if indicated
protocols.notes: No specific antidote. Supportive care | Haemodialysis indications: Li >4mmol/L (acute), Li >2.5mmol/L (chronic), severe symptoms, renal failure | Do NOT give diuretics (increases lithium retention) | Whole bowel irrigation if sustained-release preparation | Monitor renal function, thyroid function | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like presentation possible | Seizures: Benzodiazepines
protocols.notes_updates: No antidote | Haemodialysis: Li >4mmol/L acute, Li >2.5mmol/L chronic, severe symptoms, renal failure | Normal saline IV hydration | Do NOT use diuretics (worsens lithium retention) | Whole bowel irrigation for sustained-release preparations | Monitor renal and thyroid function | Seizures: benzodiazepines | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like state possible

diagnostic_criteria.features: Tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures, renal failure, hypothyroidism, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus

management_steps.details: If indicated by level or clinical status

protocols.adult_dose: Sodium Bicarbonate: 1-2mmol/kg IV bolus, then add 100mmol to 1L D5W at 1.5x maintenance | Haemodialysis if indicated
protocols.notes: Sodium Bicarbonate: urinary alkalinization (goal urine pH 7.5-8.5) | Haemodialysis indications: salicylate >100mg/dL (acute), >60mg/dL (chronic), severe acidosis, CNS symptoms, pulmonary oedema, renal failure | Activated charcoal if <1hr | Hypokalaemia common - replace K+ | Monitor blood gases, electrolytes, salicylate levels q2h | Caution: pulmonary oedema from fluid overload
protocols.notes_updates: Sodium Bicarbonate: 1-2mmol/kg IV bolus, then infusion in D5W at 1.5x maintenance rate | Goal urine pH: 7.5-8.5 | Haemodialysis: salicylate >100mg/dL acute, >60mg/dL chronic, severe acidosis, CNS symptoms, pulmonary oedema, renal failure | Activated charcoal <1hr | Replace K+ (hypokalaemia common) | Monitor levels q2h, blood gases, electrolytes | Caution pulmonary oedema from fluid overload | Respiratory alkalosis -> metabolic acidosis progression

diagnostic_criteria.features: Tinnitus, vertigo, nausea, vomiting, hyperventilation, fever, sweating, metabolic acidosis, coagulopathy, pulmonary oedema

management_steps.details: If indicated by level or clinical status

protocols.adult_dose: 100% Oxygen via NRB mask (or intubated: 100% FiO2) | Hyperbaric O2 if indicated
protocols.notes: 100% O2 reduces half-life from 320min (room air) to 80min | Hyperbaric O2 indications: COHb >25%, pregnancy (COHb >15%), loss of consciousness, severe metabolic acidosis, ongoing cardiac ischaemia, neurological deficits | Carboxyhaemoglobin level NOT reliable after O2 started (drops rapidly) | Pulse CO-oximetry if available | Check for myocardial injury (troponin, ECG) | Cerebral oedema: mannitol/hypertonic saline | Normal COHb <5% (smokers up to 10%)
protocols.notes_updates: 100% O2: half-life 320min -> 80min | Hyperbaric O2: COHb >25%, pregnancy with COHb >15%, LOC, severe acidosis, cardiac ischaemia, neuro deficits | COHb unreliable after O2 started | Check troponin, ECG for cardiac injury | Cerebral oedema: mannitol/hypertonic saline | Normal COHb <5% (<10% smokers) | Pulse CO-oximetry useful | Delayed neurological sequelae can occur days later

diagnostic_criteria.features: Headache, dizziness, confusion, syncope, seizures, coma, cherry-red skin (late), myocardial ischaemia

management_steps.details: NRB mask or intubate if needed

protocols.adult_dose: Hydroxocobalamin: 5g IV over 15min (repeat once if needed) | OR Cyanide Antidote Kit: Amyl Nitrite inhalation + Sodium Nitrite 300mg IV + Sodium Thiosulphate 12.5g IV
protocols.paediatric_dose: Hydroxocobalamin: 70mg/kg IV (max 5g) | Sodium Nitrite: 4-10mg/kg IV | Sodium Thiosulphate: 400mg/kg IV
protocols.notes: Hydroxocobalamin preferred (forms cyanocobalamin) - safer profile | Cyanide Antidote Kit: nitrites form methaemoglobin which binds cyanide; thiosulphate converts cyanide to thiocyanate | Hydroxocobalamin does NOT cause methaemoglobinaemia (safer in smoke inhalation with CO poisoning) | Check lactate (elevated in cyanide toxicity) | Supportive care, 100% O2 | Sources: smoke inhalation, industrial exposure, certain plants/seeds (apricot pits)
protocols.notes_updates: Hydroxocobalamin: 5g IV over 15min (preferred, can repeat once) | Cyanide Antidote Kit alternative: Amyl Nitrite inhalation + Sodium Nitrite 300mg IV + Sodium Thiosulphate 12.5g IV | Hydroxocobalamin preferred in smoke inhalation (no methaemoglobin formation - safer with CO) | Cyanide Kit causes methaemoglobinaemia | Check lactate (elevated) | 100% O2 | Sources: smoke inhalation, industrial, plants/seeds
protocols.antidote_treatment: Hydroxocobalamin (preferred) or Cyanide Antidote Kit (Sodium Nitrite + Sodium Thiosulphate)

diagnostic_criteria.features: Headache, confusion, seizures, coma, metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate, tachycardia, hypotension, cherry-red venous blood
diagnostic_criteria.sources: Smoke inhalation, industrial exposure, plants/seeds (apricot pits, cassava)

management_steps.details: Lactate, methaemoglobin if cyanide kit used

protocols.adult_dose: Fomepizole: Loading 15mg/kg IV, then 10mg/kg IV 12hrly x4, then 15mg/kg IV 12hrly | OR Ethanol infusion | Haemodialysis if indicated
protocols.notes: Fomepizole is antidote of choice (inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase) | Ethanol alternative if fomepizole unavailable | Haemodialysis: severe acidosis (pH <7.25), electrolyte disturbances, visual symptoms (methanol), renal failure (ethylene glycol), level >50mg/dL | Methanol: check visual acuity, fundoscopy (optic disc hyperaemia) | Ethylene glycol: calcium oxalate crystals in urine, hypocalcaemia | Both cause elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis with elevated osmolar gap | Folic acid (methanol) or Thiamine/Pyridoxine (ethylene glycol) as adjuncts
protocols.notes_updates: Fomepizole: 15mg/kg loading, then 10mg/kg 12hrly x4, then 15mg/kg 12hrly | Ethanol alternative if fomepizole unavailable | Haemodialysis: pH <7.25, electrolyte disturbances, visual symptoms, renal failure, level >50mg/dL | Methanol: visual acuity, fundoscopy, folic acid adjunct | Ethylene glycol: urine crystals, hypocalcaemia, thiamine/pyridoxine adjunct | Elevated anion gap + osmolar gap in both

diagnostic_criteria.features: Inebriation without smell of ethanol, elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis, elevated osmolar gap
diagnostic_criteria.methanol_specific: Visual disturbances, blindness, optic disc hyperaemia
diagnostic_criteria.ethylene_glycol_specific: Calcium oxalate crystals in urine, hypocalcaemia, renal failure

management_steps.action: Check anion gap, osmolar gap, ABG

protocols.adult_dose: Honey (if >1 year old): 10ml q10min until removal | OR Sucralfate 1-2g
protocols.notes: MEDICAL EMERGENCY - risk of oesophageal perforation within 2 hours | Immediate ENT/surgical removal if in oesophagus | Honey or Sucralfate can neutralise alkaline injury (if ingestion <12hrs and >1 year old) | X-ray to locate battery (AP and lateral) | If in stomach and asymptomatic: observe, repeat X-ray in 4 days if not passed | If in oesophagus: emergent removal (within 2 hours) | If symptomatic after passing to stomach: removal | Do NOT induce emesis | Do NOT check pH with pH paper (false negative) | Size >20mm = higher risk | Check for second battery or coin
protocols.notes_updates: EMERGENCY: oesophageal perforation risk within 2hrs | Honey 10ml q10min if >1yr and <12hrs since ingestion | Sucralfate 1-2g alternative | Immediate removal if in oesophagus | X-ray AP + lateral to locate | If in stomach + asymptomatic: observe, repeat X-ray in 4 days | If symptomatic: remove regardless of location | Do NOT induce emesis | Do NOT use pH paper | Size >20mm = higher risk | Check for multiple ingested objects
protocols.antidote_treatment: Honey or Sucralfate (adjunctive, not substitute for removal)

diagnostic_criteria.symptoms: Drooling, dysphagia, chest pain, respiratory distress, vomiting

management_steps.details: Observe, check stool, repeat X-ray in 4 days if not passed
